coredata.dc:title: Effect of carbon dot on preparation of dual-functional Janus particles via photo-initiated seed swelling polymerization
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yunjia
Zhao, Yashuai
Ma, Shujuan
Bai, Qingyan
Wang, Haiping
Bo, Chunmiao
Ou, Junjie
coredata.dc:description: With the development of industry, the detection and removal of heavy metal ions from water has received much attention. Janus particles are distinguished from many other materials by their unique structure and functionalization. We proposed a simple photo-initiated seed swelling polymerization method for preparing carbon dot-coated dual-functional Janus particles (Janus@CD), using glycyl methacrylate (GMA) as monomer and ethylene dimethacrylate (EDMA) as crosslinker. In order to modulate the particle morphology during the phase separation process, carbon dot (CD) was added to the reaction system. The results demonstrated that the presence of CD had a significant effect on the shape of Janus particles. Furthermore, Janus@CD could be directly applied in the adsorption and detection for MnO4 −. The limit of detection (LOD) was 6.39 nmol L−1, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 23.9 mg g−1, in line with the Langmuir adsorption model. The adsorption equilibrium reached within 30 min. The results demonstrated that dual-functional Janus@CD would present great potential in the detection and enrichment for metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Janus particle
Seed-swelling photoinitiated polymerization
Dual-functional material

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Automatic specific surface area and micropore pore analysis
Design and fabrication of Janus particles
Determination of fluorescence selectivity and sensitivity of Janus@CD-3 to ions
Possible mechanism of CD fluorescence quenching
Adsorption properties of Janus@CD-3 for MnO4 −

Ou, as well as the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22064013) to C. Bo.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthetic procedures of Janus particles.
SEM images of (a, b) J-2, (c, d) Janus@CD-2 and (e, f) J-3 using dodecanol/decanol (1/1, v/v) as porogenic agents.
SEM images of (a, b) J-6, (c, d) Janus@CD-4 and (e, f) J-7 using dodecanol/cyclohexanol (1/1, v/v) as porogenic agents.
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) XPS spectra of Janus@CD-3 and high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s and (e) O 1s.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum and (b) fluorescence intensity at 350 nm of Janus@CD-3 with 21 kinds of ions. (c) Fluorescence emission spectrum of Janus@CD-3 at different MnO4 − concentrations and (d) linear response curve for detection of MnO4 −.
Adsorption (a) isotherm and (d) kinetics curves, and fitting by the (b) Langmuir model, (c) Freundlich model, (e) pseudo-first-order model and (f) pseudo-second-order model of Janus@CD-3 for MnO4 −.
Janus particles prepared under different conditions.
Comparison of a few materials for fluorescent detection of MnO4 −.
Comparison of several reported materials for adsorption of MnO4 −.

head.ce:title: Effect of carbon dot on preparation of dual-functional Janus particles via photo-initiated seed swelling polymerization

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the development of industry, the detection and removal of heavy metal ions from water has received much attention. Janus particles are distinguished from many other materials by their unique structure and functionalization. We proposed a simple photo-initiated seed swelling polymerization method for preparing carbon dot-coated dual-functional Janus particles (Janus@CD), using glycyl methacrylate (GMA) as monomer and ethylene dimethacrylate (EDMA) as crosslinker. In order to modulate the particle morphology during the phase separation process, carbon dot (CD) was added to the reaction system. The results demonstrated that the presence of CD had a significant effect on the shape of Janus particles. Furthermore, Janus@CD could be directly applied in the adsorption and detection for MnO4 −. The limit of detection (LOD) was 6.39 nmol L−1, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 23.9 mg g−1, in line with the Langmuir adsorption model. The adsorption equilibrium reached within 30 min. The results demonstrated that dual-functional Janus@CD would present great potential in the detection and enrichment for metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasingly diverse and complex application environments require materials with multiple functions [1]. Therefore, particles with spherical and anisotropic surface chemistry have been extensively developed, which was named as Janus material. The word “Janus” is derived from the two-faced Roman god and is often adopted to describe materials with an asymmetrical form [2–6]. A large number of asymmetrically shaped microspheres such as snowman, dumbbell, half-raspberry and oval have been prepared by microfluidics [7], self-assembly [8], emulsion polymerization [9], Pickering emulsions [10], seed-mediated polymerization [11,12] and so on. Because of their unique structure, Janus particles give rise to great potential for the construction and design of multifunctional materials, especially for detection and sensing [13]. Antoni and his colleagues [14] developed an enzyme-functionalized Janus gold–mesoporous-silica nanoparticles via electrostatic attachment of oligonucleotides to the surface of aminated silica particles and applied it in fluorescence detection of urea in human blood. Alfredo et al. [34] successfully applied Janus particles composed of gold nanoparticles and silica as a base, with modified biotin on their surface, in the electrochemical detection of hydrogen peroxide. Recently, our group [15–18] prepared snowman-shaped, raspberry-shaped and dumbbell-shaped Janus particles via photo-initiated seed-mediated polymerization, which were modified via surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization and applied them in the adsorption of Cu2+ with satisfactory results. As mentioned above, Janus particles exhibit unrivaled potential and importance due to their unique asymmetry and versatility. They can be designed and prepared to simultaneously possess different physical, chemical or biological properties, making them critical in a variety of fields, especially in detection, sensing, catalysis and biomedicine.

Manganese is an essential trace element for humans and other organisms [19]. Among them, Mn(VII) is the most dangerous to human health. Long-term excessive intake of Mn(VII) can lead to a number of diseases [20], such as DNA mutations [21] and abnormalities in the nervous system [22]. It is necessary to find a simple, sensitive and selective analytical method for the detection of Mn(VII). Carbon dot (CD) is an emerging carbon-based nanomaterial [23] and has received a great deal of attention for its strong and tunable photoluminescence [24,25]. In addition, CD possesses a wealth of functional groups on their surface [26], leading to a wide range of applications in catalysis [27,28], detection [29,30], and biomedicine [27,31]. In recent years, novel CD-containing materials such as hydrogel [32] and metal-organic framework [33] combined the advantages of them and exhibited excellent properties. Pradthana and his colleagues [34] combined N, S-CD and metal hydroxides to prepare a nanoprobe for sensitive detection of tetracycline in milk and water. More challengingly, the combination of CDs with other materials showed impressive results, especially in monitoring metal ions. Ngo [35] combined CD and chitin nanocrystals (ChNC) to prepare an effective fluorescent CD/ChNC composite adsorbent. This adsorbent was applied in the simultaneous detection and removal of chromium (IV) and cobalt (II) ions from wastewater, exhibiting high selectivity and sensitivity to Cr6+ and Cr2+ and high adsorption capacities. Recently, our group [36,37] grafted CD with sulfhydryl groups onto polymeric microspheres via “click chemistry”and applied them in the detection and enrichment of metal ions. As a result, it is possible to combine CD with Janus particles to utilize the advantages of both to create a novel material with multifunctionality, high selectivity and sensitivity. The development of such materials is important for environmental monitoring, pollutant removal, biomedicine and catalysis.

In this work, monodisperse poly(glycidyl methacrylate) (poly(GMA)) particle was selected as seed to prepare CD-coated Janus particles via photo-initiated seed swelling polymerization. Glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) and ethylene dimethacrylate (EDMA) were selected as monomer and cross-linking agent, respectively, and dodecanol and paraxylene were added as porogenic agents. The effect of CD on the morphology of Janus particles was investigated, and the CD-coated semi-raspberry-like Janus particles, named Janus@CD, was successfully prepared and then applied in the detection and enrichment of MnO4 −, and the satisfied detection result and adsorption performance were obtained.

EDMA, GMA, 2,2-dimethoxy-2-phenylacetophenone (DMPA), L-tryptophan and ethylenediamine were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ethanol, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), dodecanol, decanol, paraxylene, cyclohexanol, propanol and KMnO4 were purchased from Damao (Tianjin, China). Standard solutions of ions (Zn2+, Ca2+, Li+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+, Gd3+, Ce3+, MnO4 −, H2PO4 −, AlO4 −, MoO4 2−, IO3 −, WO4 2− and Zr4+) were prepared with ultrapure water from respective metal salts of ZnCl2, CaCl2, ZrCl4, LiCl, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, KCl, Mg(NO3)2, NaCl, Pb(NO3)2, GdCl3, Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, NaH2PO4, NaAlO4, Na2MoO4, NaIO3 and Na2WO4, which were also from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). The above reagents were not further purified. The water used in the experiment was of ultrapure.
SEM model JEM-7500F (JEOL-Japan) was used to observe the morphology of Janus particles and poly(GMA) seeds.
The CD solution was diluted in anhydrous ethanol and sonicated for 30 min, after which it was dropped on a copper mesh and the morphology was observed by HT7700 TEM (JEOL-Japan).

The samples were mixed with KBr at a ratio of 1:100 in a mortar and pestle, and then pressed under a pressure of 10 MPa, and the surface functional groups were analyzed by FT-IR of Shimadzu Corporation (Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Automatic specific surface area and micropore pore analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The materials were degassed at 120 °C for 6 h and then analyzed with ASAP2020HD88 automatic specific surface area and micropore pore analysis (Max, USA).
The material was analyzed with an ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA).
The fluorescence spectrum of the material and adsorption data were analyzed by LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), integration time of 2 s, slit of 5 nm.
The zeta potential of the material was measured on a Zetasizer Nano (Malvern, UK).

CD was prepared via one-step hydrothermal polymerization. Typically, l-tryptophan (0.40 g) and ethylenediamine (0.13 mL) were dissolved into 30 mL water, and then transferred to a 75 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After heated at 160 °C for 6 h, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The obtained mixture was filtered with 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed against a 3000 Da cellulose dialysis membrane for 24 h to obtain CD.

Janus@CD was prepared by a photo-initiated seed swelling polymerization process. In general, monodisperse poly(GMA) seeds were prepared by dispersion polymerization. First, PVP (2 g), AIBN (0.2 g), GMA (10 mL) and ethanol (80 mL) were added to the round-bottomed flask. The solids were completely dissolved by ultrasound and then the reaction was conducted under a nitrogen atmosphere at 70 °C for 8 h. The product was centrifuged, washed three times with ethanol and then dispersed in SDS solution (0.2 %) to obtain a solution of poly(GMA) seeds with a density of 90 mg mL−1.

Second, 9 mL aqueous solution composed of PVA (5 %) and SDS (0.2 %) were added to a 50 mL of tube. An organic phase consisting of 0.25 mL monomer (GMA), 0.11 mL crosslinker (EDMA), porogenic agent (0.18 mL dodecanol and 0.18 mL paraxylene) was mixed with the aqueous PVA-SDS solution. The photo-initiator (30 mg DMPA) was dissolved in the organic phase. With the aid of ultrasonication, the organic phase and water phase was mixed to obtain an oil-in-water emulsified solution. The emulsion was mixed with poly(GMA) seed suspension and added to a quartz conical flask.

Finally, the quartz conical flask was shaken in a light-induced oscillatory incubator at 25 °C for 20 h with 200 r min−1 to swell the poly(GMA) seeds in the presence of the organic phase. After swelling of the seeds, 0.5 mL of concentrated CD (20 mg mL−1) solution was added and oscillated for 2 h. After that, the reaction was carried out for 2 h with ultraviolet UV light at 365 nm. The product was collected by centrifugation, washed three times with water and ethanol respectively, and then lyophilized vacuum for 24 h to obtain Janus@CD.

Dissolved a certain amount of KMnO4 into ultrapure water to prepare mother solution (1 mmol L−1) for future experiment. A series of standard solutions with the concentration of 10–500 μmol L−1 were acquired. Then, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 270 nm with these standard solutions to obtain the standard curve. In order to study the adsorption properties of Janus@CD isothermal adsorption and kinetic adsorption experiments were performed.

In isothermal adsorption experiment, 8 mg of materials were added into 25 mL of KMnO4 standard solution with different concentrations (20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 μmol L−1). The mixture was oscillated at 25 °C, 200 r min−1 for 2 h. The sample was obtained by 0.22 μm filtration membrane. The concentration of KMnO4 was measured by fluorescence spectrometry at 270 nm. The adsorption capacity (Q e) was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Q e = V (C 0 - C e) / m
where V (L) is the volume of KMnO4 solution; C 0 and C e (mg L−1) are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of KMnO4 in the adsorbed solution, respectively, and m (g) is the mass of the adsorbed material.

Isothermal adsorption curve shows the relationship between different concentration and adsorption capacity at the same temperature. Langmuir adsorption isotherm model and Freundlich adsorption isothermal model were used to study the results of isothermal adsorption.

The Langmuir adsorption isotherm model is shown by the following:
(2)
C e / Q e = C e / Q max + 1 / (K L Q max)
where Q max (mg g−1) is theoretical maximum adsorption capacity and K L(L mg−1) is the Langmuir constant.

The Freundlich adsorption isothermal model is an empirical formula which considers that the surface of adsorbent has uneven surface energy. The Freundlich model is shown by the following:
(3) lnQ e = lnK f + 1/n (lnC e)
where K f (mg g−1) is the Freundlich constant and n is the intensity of the adsorption.

In kinetic adsorption experiments, 5 mg of materials were added to 10 mL of KMnO4 standard solution (100 mg L−1). The mixture was oscillated at 25 °C, 200 r min−1 for 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 60, 90 and 120 min. Then, the samples were treated and tested using the same method as the isotherm adsorption experiment. Kinetic adsorption represents the change of adsorption capacity over time. It is generally explained by pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order dynamics models. The pseudo-first-order dynamics models are shown by the following:
(4) ln(Q e - Q t) = lnQ e - k 1 t
where Q t (mg g−1) represents the adsorption capacity at different time; k 1 is the pseudo-first-order kinetic model constant and t (min) is the adsorption time.

The pseudo-second-order dynamics models are shown by the following:
(5)
t / Q t = 1 / (k 2 Q e 2) + t / Q e
where k 2 is the pseudo-second-order kinetic model constant.

Material suspensions (0.2 mg mL−1) were prepared by dispersing a certain amount of Janus@CD in water. Different concentrations (10–100 nmol L−1) of KMnO4 standard solutions were also prepared for fluorescence detection. The solution of KMnO4 (9 mL) with different concentrations was mixed with Janus@CD suspension (1 mL), and its fluorescence intensity was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of Janus particles
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, Janus particles prepared by seed emulsion polymerization require heating to complete the process. During this process, the seed particles swell, causing them to become larger, and the cross-linked network of the seed particles becomes compressed. Phase separation occurred due to contraction of polymer chains after thermal initiation of polymerization via elastic release, resulting in the formation of Janus particles [38]. However, the preparation of Janus particles via thermal initiation polymerization is relatively time-consuming [39]. This process usually takes 20 h or more [40]. Zhang et al. proposed a hypothesis of producing Janus particles via photoinitiation mode [41]. Inspired by Zhang et al., our group [15] prepared Janus particles via photoinitiation polymerization within only 15 min of UV light irradiation.

Herein, the fabrication of Janus particles was schematically presented in Fig. 1 . First, monodisperse poly(GMA) seeds with an average diameter of 1.55 μm were acquired via dispersion polymerization (Fig. S1). An oil phase was composed of a certain amount of GMA (monomer), EDMA (cross-linker) and organic porogenic agents, which was mixed with an aqueous phase containing PVA and SDS in turn to acquire an oil-in-water emulsion with the aid of ultrasound. After that, a certain amount of CD solution was added and shaken to disperse it evenly in the emulsion. Finally, the swelled seeds were irradiated with UV light at 365 nm to cause polymerization reaction, and Janus@CD particles were obtained.

In our previous report [16], the addition of dodecanol as a porogenic agent into reaction system could generate semi-raspberry-like Janus particles. On this basis, the effect of the addition of CD solution on the morphology of Janus particles was investigated by selecting dodecanol as the sole porogenic agent (J-1, Table 1 ). The rugby-shaped particles (J-1) with an average particle size of 2.4 μm were prepared, as shown in Figs. S2a and b. Considering that the added CD solution would dilute the aqueous phase, an equal volume of water was added to the reaction system as a control group (Janus@CD-1, Table 1). As presented in Figs. S2c and d, the rugby-shaped Janus@CD-1 particles with an average size of 2.1 μm were acquired, which was slightly smaller than that of J-1.

The effect of binary porogenic agents on the morphology of Janus particles was systematically investigated. Using dodecanol/decanol (1/1, v/v) as porogenic agents, the as-synthesized J-2 exhibited rugby-shaped morphology with an average particle size of 3.1 μm, as presented in Fig. 2 a and b. Obviously, small particles were stuck on J-2, deducing the self-aggregation of monomers. When the CD solution was added, a large number of semi-raspberry-like Janus@CD-2 with an average particle size of 2.9 μm were acquired (Fig. 2c and d), while the resulting particles (J-3) with an average size of 3.9 μm became hemispherical(Fig. 2e and f) when aqueous solution was added, and many small particles still adhered to one side of them. Selecting dodecanol/paraxylene (Fig. S3) and dodecanol/propanol (Fig. S4) as binary porogenic agents, the as-acquired Janus particles exhibited similar morphology to that fabricated with dodecanol/decanol as porogenic agents. As depicted in Figs. S3c, d, S4c and d, Janus@CD-3 and Janus@CD-5 exhibited semi-raspberry morphology, but with average particle sizes of 5.2 μm and 4.0 μm, respectively, slightly larger than Janus@CD-2. As shown in Fig. 3 c and d, Janus@CD-4 did not exhibit semi-raspberry-like morphology, but an intermediate state of transition to semi-raspberry-like. From these results, it is speculated that the formation of semi-raspberry-like Janus@CD was related to the polarity of binary porogenic agent.

Based on the above results, we proposed a mechanism for rapid preparation of CD-coated semi-raspberry-like Janus particles via photoinitiation mode. As shown in Fig. 1, the monomer (GMA) and organic solvent enter the seeds to reach a swelling equilibrium and generate a new oil-water interface inside the seeds. Then, under UV irradiation, GMA underwent free radical polymerization to form a cross-linked network on the surface. Finally, the entire droplet was extruded from the seed particle driven by elastic contraction force to form small particles that adhere onto the surface of Janus particles, but this process did not result in the formation of semi-raspberry-like Janus particles. The addition of CD changed this process. Prior to photoinitiation, CD containing amino groups was added into the reaction system, then the poly(GMA) seeds, CD containing amino groups and the self-polymerized particles would self-assembly generate semi-raspberry-like Janus particles. In this process, the CD containing amino groups reacted with the epoxy groups on the surface of poly(GMA) seeds, resulting in the coating of CD on the surface of poly(GMA) seeds. When the self-polymerizing droplets were squeezed out from the poly(GMA) seeds, they reacted with the CD on the poly(GMA) surface due to the abundance of epoxy groups, forming strong chemical bonds. This prevents the self-polymerizing monomer particles from the surface of Janus particles, ultimately forming semi-raspberry-like Janus particles.

Compared with Janus particles of other morphologies, semi-raspberry-like Janus particles possessed relatively larger specific surface area and more active sites, and showed better performance in detection and adsorption [15]. Therefore, we selected Janus@CD-3 (Table 1), which exhibited higher yields, for further application. As shown in Fig. S5a, CD was prepared via a hydrothermal method using l-tryptophan and ethylenediamine as raw materials to emit blue fluorescence. The TEM images of CD are given in Figs. S5b–d. The CD was well dispersed and nearly spherical with an average particle size of 2.75 nm. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption curve and pore size distribution of J-4 and Janus@CD-3 are shown in Figs. S6a and b, the pore volume and specific surface area of them were lower than 0.3 cm3 g−1 and 35 m2 g−1, respectively. As shown in Figs. S6c–e, Janus@CD-3 could emit blue fluorescence when illuminated with excitation light at a wavelength of 405 nm. This confirms that CD successfully coated onto the surface of Janus@CD-3. The specific surface area, pore volume and average pore size data of J-4 and Janus@CD-3 are shown in Table S1. The specific surface area of Janus@CD-3 increased from 11.7 to 33.3 m2 g−1 compared to J-4, and the average pore size was 22.4 nm. Fig. 4 a presents the FT-IR spectra of J-4, Janus@CD-3 and CD. Two absorption peaks at 3400 and 3486 cm−1 emerged in CD, which was characteristic absorption peaks of amino group. The absorption peak at 740 cm−1 belonged to the characteristic peak of bending vibrations of C–H bond in the benzene ring skeleton in CD. The two peaks appearing at 1450 and 1600 cm−1 were skeletal vibrations of the benzene ring. These chemical groups were derived from l-tryptophan and ethylenediamine. The peak at 1740 cm−1, which was characteristic of CO, appeared in both J-4 and Janus@CD-3. This was explained by the polymerization of GMA and EDMA. The characteristic peaks at 3400 and 3486 cm−1 of amino group could be clearly observed in the Janus@CD-3 containing CD, but not in the J-4. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 1420 cm−1 belonged to the C–N stretching vibration peak in Janus@CD-3. As shown in Fig. 4b, XPS can measure the chemical groups and composition of the material surface. It is obvious that the Janus@CD-3 surface consists of abundant C, N and O elements, while no significant N elements are observed from J-4 (Fig. S7). The high-resolution XPS spectra for Janus@CD-3 are shown in Fig. 4c–e. The peaks at 284.8, 286.2, 286.9 and 288.7 eV in the C1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) were the characteristic adsorption of C–C, C–O, C–N and CO, respectively. The peak at 399.3 eV in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) was the characteristic adsorption of pyrrolic N. The peaks at 532.9 and 531.6 eV in the O1s spectrum were the characteristic adsorption of CO and C–O, respectively. These results further indicated that Janus@CD-3 was successfully prepared.

Additionally, to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the morphology of Janus particles, they were characterized by zeta potential and particle size distribution. As shown in Table S2, the mean zeta potential values for all Janus particles are well lower than −40 mV, indicating strong electrostatic repulsion between the particles and excellent electrostatic stability [42,43]. Through particle size distribution measurements of Janus particles, the results showed that most PDI values were below 0.3 (Table S2), indicating that the particle size distribution of Janus particles is relatively uniform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of fluorescence selectivity and sensitivity of Janus@CD-3 to ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to validate the fluorescence selectivity of CD, the influence of different ions on fluorescence properties of CD was measured. Twenty-one metal ion solutions (100 μmol L−1) were added into the Janus@CD-3 suspension (0.2 mg L−1) to determine its fluorescence intensity, respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 5a and b . Obviously, it could be found that the blank samples of Janus@CD-3 exhibited high fluorescence intensity, which is conducive to selective detection experiments. When aqueous solutions of Li+, K+, Na+, or Ca2+ was added, the fluorescence intensity was slightly enhanced. The fluorescence intensity was not changed significantly when aqueous solutions of Cr2+, Zr4+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ce2+ H2PO4 −, AlO4 −, MoO4 2−, WO4 2−and IO3 − were added. When aqueous solutions of Cu2+ and Fe3+ were added, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly, but the degree of quenching was less than 27 %. In contrast, the addition of MnO4 − aqueous solution resulted in a significant decrease of fluorescence intensity, with the degree of quenching greater than 49 %. In addition, a mixture of MnO4 − and other metal ions were added to the suspension of Janus@CD-3 to investigate the resistance of the material to interference. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescent quenching of MnO4 − was not significantly affected by the addition of other metal ions. These results demonstrated that the Janus@CD-3 exhibited excellent selectivity for MnO4 − in fluorescence detection.

To investigate the fluorescence detection ability of Janus@CD-3 for MnO4 −, fluorescence quenching experiments were performed with different concentrations of MnO4 −. Stern-Volmer equation was used to quantify the degree of Janus@CD-3 fluorescence quenching, as described the following:
(6)
F 0 / F = 1 + K sv [c]
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity with and without addition of MnO4 −, respectively, K sv is the fluorescence quenching constant, and [c] is the concentration of MnO4 −.

As shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity of Janus@CD-3 decreased gradually with an increase of MnO4 − concentration ranging from 10 to 100 nmol L−1. It could be observed from Fig. 5d that the Janus@CD-3 fluorescence intensity was linearly correlated with the concentration of MnO4 − (linear correlation coefficient R = 0.996), and the fitting equation was F 0/F = 0.0035[c]+0.9786. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated from LOD = 3σ/s, where σ is the relative standard deviation of fluorescence intensity without the addition of MnO4 − and s is the slope of calibration curve. Then, the LOD of Janus@CD-3 for MnO4 − was calculated to be 6.39 nmol L−1. In addition, when CD was applied to detect MnO4 −, the linear range was 0–100 μmol L−1 (the linear correlation coefficient R = 0.996). As a result, it could be seen that the LOD of Janus@CD-3 for MnO4 − in water samples is significantly lower than that of CD. Thus, the fluorescence analysis method for MnO4 − in water samples based on Janus@CD-3 exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity and could quantitatively detect MnO4 − in water. Table 2 lists several reported materials with fluorescent properties to selectively detect MnO4 −. It could be found that the Janus@CD-3 prepared by us exhibited high sensitivity and low LOD for MnO4 − in water. Furthermore, fluorescence detection was also performed on Fe3+ and Cu2+, which exhibited significant fluorescence quenching. As shown in Figs. S9a and b, the Janus@CD-3 fluorescence intensity was linearly correlated with the concentration of Fe3+, the linear correlation coefficient was 0.998 and the fitting equation was F 0/F = 0.0015[c]+0.9473. The LOD of Janus@CD-3 for Fe3+ was calculated to be 12.4 nmol L−1. Fig. S9c and d present the fluorescence intensity of Janus@CD-3, which exhibited excellent linear relationship with the concentration of Cu2+. The linear correlation coefficient was 0.998, and the fitting equation was F 0/F = 0.00101[c]+1.022. The LOD of Janus@CD-3 for Cu2+ was calculated to be 17.7 nmol L−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of CD fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CD for MnO4 − could be explained by inner filter effect (IFE) [44], which is different from that for Fe3+ and Cu2+. IFE is an important mechanism of fluorescence quenching, which is the reabsorption of excited/emitted light of fluorophore by the quencher [45]. IFE generally requires a complementary region of overlap between the absorption spectrum of absorber and the excitation or emission spectrum of fluorophore. As depicted in Fig. S10a, the CD prepared from l-tryptophan and ethylenediamine exhibited strong fluorescence emission at 350 nm with the maximal excitation at 278 nm, while MnO4 − possessed multiple absorption peaks. An overlap between the emission/excitation spectrum of CD and the absorption band of MnO4 − appeared, so that MnO4 − can not only shield the excitation light for CD but also absorb the emission light from CD, allowing efficient IFE-based fluorescence quenching. Simultaneously, the quenching mechanism of CD could also be explained by the theory of electron transfer [46]. As observed from Fig. S11, it is well known that the color of MnO4 − aqueous solution is purple, but the color of solution changes from purple to brown after adding CD. This is attributed to the transfer of negative charge from the surface of the CD onto MnO4 −, meanwhile, Mn7+ was reduced to Mn6+ or Mn4+. The electron transfer mechanism could also explain the changes in zeta potential. When CD solution was added to the MnO4 − solution system, the zeta potential of CD solution changed from −24.8 mV to −4.5 mV.

As shown in Fig. S10b, Fe3+and Cu2+ did not fulfill the conditions for IFE to occur. There are a plethora of reports [47–50] on the quenching mechanisms of CD. The addition of l-tryptophan results in the presence of abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of CD, while the inherent paramagnetic properties of Fe3+ and Cu2+ affect the charge transfer of carboxyl groups on CDs, leading to fluorescence quenching [48,51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption properties of Janus@CD-3 for MnO4 −
ce:section.ce:para: The surface functionalization of Janus particles by CD would increase active sites of the material. The CD surface contains a large number of amino groups, which can offer a strong electrostatic force with MnO4 −, thus realizing the adsorption of MnO4 − [52]. Therefore, these amino groups can also chelate Fe3+ and Cu2+, thus making the Janus@CD-3 gain the ability to adsorb Fe3+ and Cu2+. Isothermal and kinetic adsorption experiments were performed using a fluorescence spectrometer to determine the adsorption properties of Janus@CD-3.

As shown in Fig. 6 a, the adsorption properties of Janus@CD-3 was investigated in the MnO4 − concentration range of 0–500 μmol L−1. In the concentration range of 0–100 μmol L−1, the adsorption capacity of Janus@CD-3 increased rapidly with an increase of MnO4 − concentration, and even the adsorption equilibrium of Janus@CD-3 was achieved at 100 μmol L−1, and the adsorption capacity was 22.9 mg g−1. Both Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms are most classical models to describe adsorption process. The result was shown in Fig. 6b and c and Table S3. It could be observed that Langmuir adsorption isothermal model (R = 0.9982) was more appropriate for this situation than Freundlich adsorption isothermal model (R = 0.8343) in the MnO4 − adsorption on Janus@CD-3. Fig. S12a presents the adsorption properties of Janus@CD-3 was investigated in the Fe3+ concentration range of 0–500 μmol L−1. Janus@CD-3 exhibited a remarkable increase in adsorption capacity as the concentration of Fe3+ was increased in the range of 0–100 μmol L−1. At a concentration of 100 μmol L−1, the adsorption equilibrium of Janus@CD-3 was obtained, with an impressive adsorption capacity of 15.9 mg g−1. In the study, the adsorption capacity of Janus@CD-3 for Fe3+ also was analyzed using the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms, which are both classical models commonly used to describe adsorption phenomena. The results presented in Fig. S12b and c and Table S3, indicated that the Langmuir adsorption isothermal model (R = 0.9991) provided a better fit for the adsorption data compared to the Freundlich adsorption isothermal model (R = 0.4533). In Fig. S13a, we investigated the adsorption properties of Janus@CD-3 in the concentration range of Cu2+ from 0 to 500 μmol L−1. In the range of 0–200 μmol L−1, the adsorption capacity of Janus@CD-3 was rapidly increased with increasing Cu2+ concentration. Specifically, at a concentration of 200 μmol L−1, Janus@CD-3 reached adsorption equilibrium with an adsorption capacity of 13.5 mg g−1. Obviously, the Langmuir adsorption isothermal model (R = 0.9996) provided a better fit to the data compared to the Freundlich adsorption isothermal model (R = 0.8390) for the adsorption of Cu2+ on Janus@CD-3. Thus, the Langmuir model was more appropriate to describe the adsorption capacity of Janus@CD-3 for Cu2+. In summary, Langmuir adsorption isotherm model assumes that the surface properties of the adsorbent were homogeneous, and the adsorption of Janus@CD-3 follows a monolayer adsorption mechanism, as predicted by the Langmuir model, rather than a heterogeneous adsorption mechanism described by the Freundlich model.

Kinetic adsorption is one of the important means to investigate the adsorption properties of materials. As shown in Fig. 6d, the kinetic adsorption of MnO4 − on Janus@CD-3 was performed in 0–120 min. It could be found that the adsorption capacity of Janus@CD-3 was rapidly increased within 20 min, and the adsorption capacity reached saturation within 30 min. In addition to studying the adsorption properties of Janus@CD-3 on MnO4 −, we also investigated the kinetic adsorption of Fe3+ and Cu2+ on Janus@CD-3 (Figs. S12d and S13d). In both cases, the adsorption equilibrium was reached within a relatively short period of time, specifically within 30 min. This suggests that Janus@CD-3 exhibits efficient and rapid adsorption of both MnO4 −, Fe3+ and Cu2+. The kinetics of adsorption for MnO4 −, Fe3+ and Cu2+ on Janus@CD-3 could be further explored to understand the underlying mechanisms and optimize the adsorption process. The results of kinetic adsorption were analyzed by pseudo-first-order (Fig. 6e, Fig. S12e and Fig. S13e) and pseudo-second-order (Fig. 6f, Fig. S12f and Fig. S13f) kinetic models and shown in Table S4. The two kinetic models were compared, namely the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order dynamics models, we analyzed their suitability in describing the adsorption process of MnO4 − on Janus@CD-3. The results revealed that the pseudo-second-order dynamics model (R = 0.9974) provided a significantly better fit to the experimental data compared to the pseudo-first-order dynamics model (R = 0.5193). This indicates that the adsorption of MnO4 −, Fe3+ and Cu2+ on Janus@CD-3 follows a chemical adsorption process, as described by the pseudo-second-order kinetics model. The pseudo-second-order kinetics model suggests that the rate-limiting step of the adsorption process involves the formation of a chemical bond between the MnO4 − and Janus@CD-3. This model is more appropriate in depicting the actual adsorption behavior and provides valuable insights for further understanding the adsorption mechanism of MnO4 − on Janus@CD-3. Table 3 shows a comparison of MnO4 − adsorption by several materials. It could be found that the adsorption capacity of SiO2@SFNO was higher than that of our synthesized Janus@CD-3, but the equilibrium time of Janus@CD-3 was faster than that of SiO2@SFNO [53]. Janus@CD-3 exhibits promising applications for rapid adsorption and detection of MnO4 − in water samples.

In summary, we successfully prepared carbon dot-coated dual-functional Janus particles through a one-pot seed swelling method initiated by UV light. We further discussed the influence of different porogen types on the morphology and found that the monomers, CD, and swollen seeds could self-assemble into semi-raspberry-like Janus particles in a less polar porogen system. It is worth noting that this Janus@CD material was prepared under UV light irradiation without the need for heating, making the experimental conditions more stable, environmentally friendly, and efficient. By evaluating the fluorescence properties of Janus@CD, the material exhibited stable fluorescence quenching for MnO4 −, indicating a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and MnO4 − concentration in the range of 10–100 nmol L−1. The CD surface contains numerous hydrophilic groups, which made Janus@CD excellent dispersibility for the detection and adsorption of MnO4 − in real environments. Furthermore, by adjusting the proportions of different raw materials, the morphology of Janus particles could be controlled. This provides a new approach for fabrication of CD-integrated composite Janus particles. It is anticipated that novel dual-functional composite materials combining Janus characteristics and CD-based fluorescence detection capabilities would have broad prospects for application.

Yunjia Xu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yashuai Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Shujuan Ma: Formal analysis. Qingyan Bai: Formal analysis. Haiping Wang: Formal analysis. Chunmiao Bo: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Junjie Ou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Ou, as well as the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22064013) to C. Bo.
